evidence_quotes.quote: Best accustoms not to own your games for which you pay
“If the purchase is not owned, piracy is not theft” has been created among players
we pay for a product that can be withdrawn at any time only because the publisher says so
When the support stops, you lose all the game for which you have paid.
'I need to communicate with our server every day, or you are no longer able to play the game because we lock you permanently off our perimeters and become unusable'
The Crew, Ubisoft simply closed down a server with a single game, making it absolutely impossible to play that game when it is not connected to a server which is closed.

paragraphs.text: Would it be possible to add video games to this whole, since I am sacrificed to pay for games at the end of my day. Especially when publishers say that; “Best accustoms not to own your games for which you pay”. Such a ‘does not own what you buy’ attitude is certainly not good for the storage or purchase of video games, we pay for a product that can be withdrawn at any time only because the publisher says so, this is in no way fair to the consumer from whom the frase is situated; ‘If the purchase is not owned, piracy is not theft’ has been created among players, this sentence will be audible to every player’s lips today, and it is only to be strengthened if gaming companies are allowed to continue to draw a drum from a wall that is not necessarily a ‘live service’, deciding to destroy digital games, which for many are even an important part of childhood, teenage, or the sole source of lone fains in adulthood, and today even among seniors. If I buy a product, then the publisher should not have the authority to take it out of me, i.e. ‘When the support stops, you lose all the game for which you have paid.’ Or, ‘I need to communicate with our server every day, or you are no longer able to play the game because we lock you permanently off our perimeters and become unusable’. An example of lost games is The Crew, Ubisoft simply closed down a server with a single game, making it absolutely impossible to play that game when it is not connected to a server which is closed. Or Blizzard, which closed a game which sold more than 50 million pieces, knowing more than one trillion of income, ‘Overwatch 1’, could, at the end of the official subsidy, have published tools for players to continue playing it, either alone or between several machines, but, if not, a wall was lost and thus lost the love game to a girl, many of whom paid a lot to enjoy it. Today, the gaming industry continues only on the same footing as regards gaming, all go on a ‘live service’ basis, remove and destroy games that are love for many, and invest more in such activities simply because these publishers do not want to play old games, while at the same time publishing broken or incomplete games at full price to replace them, and even these new games have no permanent chance of playing after the end of the official support.
